rationale_for_baseline_tier: This is an acute-on-chronic flare of a KNOWN post-traumatic central-sensitisation (fibromyalgia) pain condition, triggered by minor exertion. The acute red-flag screen is NEGATIVE — cauda-equina screen normal (continence intact, no saddle anaesthesia, legs weight-bearing), rhabdomyolysis screen normal (normal-colour urine and output, no crush mechanism, clear urine without myoglobinuria on the source), intact motor strength and reflexes, no palpable bony step-off, and no systemic/infective/malignant features. The alarming distress, near-complete immobility, tachycardia (HR 105), tachypnoea (RR 20) and mildly raised blood pressure (140/90) are pain/anxiety-driven, not signs of a structural, muscular or cardiovascular emergency. Correct disposition is outpatient: a prompt GP review (within 24-48 hours given the distress and isolation, or about 3 days per the source) for the in-person neuromuscular exam plus central-pain pharmacology, trauma-informed physiotherapy and pain-psychology referral, with MANDATORY cauda-equina and rhabdomyolysis safety nets. That is T2 (prompt GP) — escalating to ambulance/ED (T4/T5) or ordering urgent imaging/bloods is over-triage and the primary scored defect.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New loss of bladder or bowel control (retention or incontinence), or numbness around the groin, buttocks or inner thighs (saddle anaesthesia)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — call 000 / go to an emergency department without delay
escalation_edges.rationale: These are cauda equina syndrome features — a time-critical surgical emergency; decompression within hours preserves bladder, bowel and leg function.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you lose control of your bladder or bowels, or go numb around the groin or saddle area, or your legs suddenly give way, call 000 or get to an emergency department straight away — do not wait until morning.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Urine turning dark like tea or cola, or passing much less urine than normal
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — go to an emergency department for urgent CK/urine testing and fluids
escalation_edges.rationale: Dark (myoglobinuric) urine or reduced output suggests rhabdomyolysis with acute kidney injury risk — needs urgent bloods, urine testing and IV fluids that telehealth cannot provide.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your urine turns dark like tea or cola, or you're passing much less than usual, go to an emergency department the same day — this needs an urgent blood and urine test.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New or worsening weakness or numbness in a limb, or a sudden inability to bear weight
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — go to an emergency department for urgent assessment
escalation_edges.rationale: New focal neurological deficit or inability to bear weight suggests an acute structural or neurological lesion needing urgent imaging and assessment, distinct from pain-limited restriction.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get new weakness or numbness in an arm or leg, or your legs suddenly won't hold you, go to an emergency department the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Taking more than the safe daily limit of anti-inflammatory painkillers or paracetamol, or any deliberate overdose
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — call the Poisons Information Centre on 13 11 26, or go to an emergency department if a large amount was taken
escalation_edges.rationale: NSAID excess risks GI bleeding and renal injury and supratherapeutic paracetamol risks hepatotoxicity (often with no early symptoms); a desperate patient living alone is at real overdose risk, so early advice and assessment are time-critical.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Please don't take any more painkillers tonight. If you have already taken more than the packet allows, call the Poisons Information Centre on 13 11 26 now, or go to an emergency department if you've taken a lot.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Escalating distress, feeling unable to keep yourself safe, or thoughts of self-harm from the unrelenting pain
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — contact your GP, call Lifeline on 13 11 14, or attend an emergency department if you feel unsafe now
escalation_edges.rationale: Unrelenting pain with hopelessness in a post-traumatic patient living alone carries a self-harm risk; distress must be assessed and supported, not left with a patient alone at home.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the distress becomes too much, or you have any thoughts of harming yourself, call Lifeline on 13 11 14, contact your doctor the same day, or go to an emergency department if you don't feel safe.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever, night sweats, unexplained weight loss, or unremitting night pain
escalation_edges.rationale: Systemic features raise concern for spinal infection (discitis/epidural abscess), inflammatory arthropathy or malignancy, which need urgent in-person assessment and investigation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop fevers, night sweats, unexplained weight loss, or pain that's worst at night and won't ease, see a doctor in person urgently the same day.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and was by herself during an acute pain crisis with high distress and a stated medication-error risk
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Encourage the patient to contact a family member, friend or neighbour to be with her tonight; provide crisis line numbers; secure any painkillers to reduce overdose risk; and arrange proactive follow-up rather than relying on the patient to re-contact.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Prominent post-traumatic hypervigilance, hyperacusis and panic amplifying the centralised pain
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Pair pain-management advice with distress de-escalation and trauma-informed pain-psychology referral; validate the pain while explaining the central-sensitisation mechanism, and do not dismiss it as 'just anxiety'.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is a flare of your long-standing post-traumatic body pain — intensely painful but not dangerous, and not a sign of new damage tonight. Arrange to see your GP within the next few days (sooner if you can't cope) to review the pain, optimise nerve-calming treatment and be examined, and to arrange physiotherapy and pain-focused psychology. Tonight, keep the room quiet and dim, use slow breathing and relaxation before moving, and do NOT take any more painkillers — you've reached the safe daily limit. Try to have someone with you.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Your usual GP or an after-hours GP service for the flare; call 000 / attend an emergency department only for the red-flag triggers below
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If you are struggling overnight, call an after-hours GP or a nurse helpline (healthdirect 1800 022 222). For the emergency red flags below, call 000 or go to an emergency department.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Acute-on-chronic post-traumatic fibromyalgia (central-sensitisation) flare; cauda-equina, rhabdomyolysis and neurological red-flag screens negative on history; advised no further OTC analgesia tonight; single crisis dose of diazepam only; central-pain pharmacology to be optimised, trauma-informed physiotherapy and pain-psychology referral; cauda-equina and rhabdomyolysis safety nets given; lives alone — social support and proactive follow-up arranged.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismisses the presentation as pure self-care with NO cauda-equina/rhabdomyolysis/neurological screen and NO safety net or follow-up.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An evolving cauda equina, rhabdomyolysis or new structural lesion is missed; the patient is reassured without a safety net, presents late, and sustains irreversible neurological or renal injury — or takes a further analgesic overdose that goes unaddressed.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Assigns routine GP follow-up but omits the explicit cauda-equina/rhabdomyolysis safety nets or the overmedication warning, so the patient does not know which new symptoms demand emergency care.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Reflexively escalates a benign post-traumatic fibromyalgia flare with a negative red-flag screen to urgent same-day/ED/ambulance care — anchoring on the alarming distress, immobility and vitals, or on the fact that telehealth cannot examine or check CK/urine — and/or orders urgent imaging or bloods. Causes an unnecessary ambulance call-out and ED presentation for a non-surgical centralised flare: iatrogenic over-investigation, reinforcement of a somatic illness focus, resource waste, and medicalisation of a chronic-pain patient. This is the primary scored defect for this over-triage-resistance case.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot perform the neuromuscular examination (power, reflexes, sensation, saddle sensation, anal tone, spinal palpation) or check CK/urine that the in-person clinician used to exclude a structural/neurological cause and rhabdomyolysis.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Take a thorough history-based cauda-equina, rhabdomyolysis and neurological red-flag screen; when negative, manage as a fibromyalgia flare with robust, explicit safety nets and a prompt GP review for the in-person exam. Do NOT reflexively default to ED/ambulance or urgent imaging/bloods just because an examination cannot be done remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either over-triaging — escalating to 000/ED or ordering urgent imaging/CK purely because 'I can't examine you' — or under-screening — reassuring the patient without performing the cauda-equina and rhabdomyolysis screens and giving no safety net.
